title: Merces
description: Merces by TACEO is a confidential token protocol implementing Private Shared State (PSS). It wraps any ERC-20 into a confidential version: balances are stored as secret shares across the TACEO MPC network, while only commitments are published on-chain. Deposits, withdrawals, and transfers are computed inside the MPC network, which produces a collaborative SNARK (CoSNARK) proving each state transition. Integrity is enforced on-chain by proof verification, so funds stay safe even if the entire MPC network is corrupted and only confidentiality rests on non-collusion.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Balances and transfer amounts are hidden: balances are held as secret shares across the TACEO MPC network, and a transfer's amount is posted only as a commitment with encrypted ciphertexts for the MPC nodes, never as plaintext on-chain. State transitions are verified by CoSNARKs, so no single party including TACEO sees what an account holds.

citations.cited_text:
Balances are held as secret shares across the TACEO Network, state
transitions are verified onchain via CoSNARKs, and no single party (including TACEO) sees what
anyone holds or sends.
Since the sender, receiver and actual token amount is hidden, they are only given as a commitment, which later will be used to verify the ZK proof produced by the MPC network.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Merces II's fully private mode hides sender, receiver, and amount — each supplied only as a commitment that the MPC-produced proof verifies, with secret shares carried in the on-chain ciphertext.

citations.cited_text: Balances are encrypted, transfers hide all parties and amounts, and every state transition is verified by a zero-knowledge proof onchain.
Since the sender, receiver and actual token amount is hidden, they are only given as a commitment, which later will be used to verify the ZK proof produced by the MPC network.
Merces II supports two privacy modes, selectable per transaction.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Each deployment wraps one specific ERC-20, and the contract holds the actual tokens backing every private balance, so observers see which token is wrapped even though balances are encrypted. The encrypted Merkle tree tracks a single token type. Supporting multiple assets within the same encrypted tree is named as future work, not a live capability.

citations.cited_text: It holds the actual ERC-20 tokens backing all private balances, stores the current commitment (Merkle root) to the encrypted balance tree
supporting multiple assets within the same encrypted Merkle tree, enabling private interactions with external DeFi contracts while preserving the proof model

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Interacting with Merces is detectable on-chain. The entry path is a deposit registered in the contract's action queue rather than an ordinary token transfer, so the user posts commitments and encrypted data to a protocol-specific contract. That registration is visibly distinct from a standard ERC-20 transaction, marking the address as a Merces user.

citations.cited_text: stores the current commitment (Merkle root) to the encrypted balance tree, and maintains an action queue where users register intents: deposits, transfers, withdrawals.
A user registers an intent, such as a private transfer, by posting commitments and encrypted data onchain.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Merces runs as a standard Solidity contract on existing EVM chains with no settlement step of its own, so finality is inherited from the host chain. A transfer also waits for the off-chain MPC round-trip: the demo reports an average user-perceived latency of about 8.4 seconds, dominated by the two on-chain transactions it requires, with the MPC computation itself sub-second. Cadence is set by the host chain plus this MPC round-trip, not a protocol-imposed challenge window.

citations.cited_text: The system runs as a standard Solidity contract, no protocol-level modifications, no custom chain, deployable wherever the EVM does.
the latency experienced by a user is comparably high (e.g., 8.4 s as depicted in the screenshot). This discrepancy arises from the block time of the underlying blockchain.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: The user holds two secrets: their host-chain wallet key, used for the public ERC-20 deposit and withdraw legs, and a separate sender secret key for the private account whose public key is registered at an index in the on-chain ID-registry. Spends prove knowledge of the sender key, not the wallet. Per-transfer ECDH keypairs that encrypt the secret shares for the MPC nodes are sampled fresh and never persisted.

citations.cited_text: The client knows the sender secret key, for which the corresponding public key is stored at the sender index in the ID-registry.
the smart contract holds a public key for each MPC party, and the user samples a fresh

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: Depositing takes one host-chain transaction with no protocol-imposed waiting period. The deposit call triggers an ERC-20 payment from the user to the contract and registers a deposit in the action queue on success. The MPC network updates the secret-shared balance off-chain afterwards — about 8.4 seconds end-to-end in the demo.

citations.cited_text: this function triggers a ERC-20 payment from the user to the on-chain contract, and upon success, registers a deposit in the action queue.
the latency experienced by a user is comparably high (e.g., 8.4 s as depicted in the screenshot). This discrepancy arises from the block time of the underlying blockchain.

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: Withdrawing is an ordinary on-chain action with no protocol-imposed waiting period. The user registers a withdraw intent in the action queue, and after the MPC network verifies it the contract releases an ERC-20 transfer to the receiver. The round-trip — register, MPC-process, verify, release — runs about 8.4 seconds end-to-end in the demo.

citations.cited_text: A withdraw only registers the intent in the action queue since we are only allowed to pay out the actual tokens if the user has enough balance.
the latency experienced by a user is comparably high (e.g., 8.4 s as depicted in the screenshot). This discrepancy arises from the block time of the underlying blockchain.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Each intent is executed by the MPC committee, which updates balances, computes the new Merkle root, and produces the validity proof. If that committee refuses an intent, the contract cannot settle it, since a withdraw only registers the intent and is verified once the network processes it. No permissionless fallback lets a user bypass the fixed executor set.

citations.cited_text: The MPC network is a set of three computing nodes that jointly hold secret shares of every user’s balance in a Merkle tree
When an action appears in the queue, the nodes collaboratively update the relevant balances, compute the new Merkle root, and produce a zero-knowledge proof that the update was done correctly.
A withdraw only registers the intent in the action queue since we are only allowed to pay out the actual tokens if the user has enough balance.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Merces depends on the TACEO MPC network. These nodes jointly hold secret shares of every user's balance and execute every transfer off-chain before the contract verifies the result. When an action appears in the queue the nodes update balances, compute the new Merkle root, and produce a validity proof, after which one node posts the proof and root on-chain. At the time of writing, the committee is a curated, closed set, so the partner set is permissioned rather than open.

citations.cited_text: The MPC network is a set of three computing nodes that jointly hold secret shares of every user’s balance in a Merkle tree
produce a zero-knowledge proof that the update was done correctly. One node then posts the proof and new root onchain.
The MPC network produces a ZK proof attesting for correct evaluation of the next item in the action queue.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Withdrawals depend entirely on the MPC network to process the queued intent and produce the validity proof. A withdraw only registers the intent in the action queue, and the tokens are paid out only once the MPC network verifies the request. The deployed Merces II articles describe no forced-exit path independent of the network — the Merces I paper mentions an optional fail-safe extension, but it is not documented as live. If the operators stall, users have no fallback.

citations.cited_text: A withdraw only registers the intent in the action queue since we are only allowed to pay out the actual tokens if the user has enough balance.
The MPC network produces a ZK proof attesting for correct evaluation of the next item in the action queue.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: The deployed Merces contract on testnet has a verified source: a standalone Solidity contract with no proxy and no upgrade mechanism, so the code cannot be changed after deployment. It has no Ownable owner or admin role — the only privileged address is the MPC poster (`onlyMpc`), which advances state through proof-verified `processMPC` calls but cannot alter the code or verifier keys. Fixed at deployment, so immutable rather than admin-controlled.

citations.cited_text: The system runs as a standard Solidity contract, no protocol-level modifications, no custom chain, deployable wherever the EVM does.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: The Merces client builds each action's cryptographic material locally: commitments to sender, receiver, and amount, encrypted secret shares for the MPC nodes, and a proof that everything was constructed honestly. These client proofs are written in Circom and evaluated with SNARKjs, so proof generation runs on the user's device rather than a hosted prover. The separate MPC proofs attest to correct state updates over secret-shared balances and are distinct from the user-authored proofs.

citations.cited_text: The user’s client (wallet or frontend) constructs the cryptographic material needed to register an action: commitments to sender, receiver, and amount
The client proofs are purely written in Circom and evaluated using SNARKjs.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: By default no single party sees balances: they are split into secret shares across MPC nodes, no node knows any balance, the contract only verifies proofs, and proving is client-side. But the warrant-gated regulator flow lets the MPC network run a scan and open a matching transaction's shares to a regulator, so a third party can inspect transaction details without the user's consent.

citations.cited_text: The MPC network is a set of three computing nodes that jointly hold secret shares of every user’s balance in a Merkle tree
It never sees plaintext balances or counterparty relationships. It only verifies proofs.
Whenever a match is found, the corresponding shares of the transaction details are opened (i.e., revealed) to the regulator.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: At the time of writing, Merces is deployed on public testnets (Arc, Base, Plasma) with a live demo, where the network has processed roughly 5 million demo transactions at about 300 TPS with single-digit-cent L2 gas. Custom deployments are available on request.

citations.cited_text: The system is deployed on the Plasma testnet.
The confidential payments network has processed ~5M demo transactions across Arc and Base testnets, sustaining ~300 TPS with single-digit-cents gas per transfer on L2.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Merces relies on Groth16 proofs over pairing-friendly elliptic curves for both client and MPC proofs, which Shor's algorithm breaks on a capable quantum computer. The client proofs use Circom and SNARKjs. Encrypted secret shares use Poseidon2 symmetric encryption with keys derived by elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman between a fresh user keypair and the MPC nodes' public keys.

citations.cited_text: The client proofs are purely written in Circom and evaluated using SNARKjs.
Since each Groth16 proof requires a large proving key, we embed the proofs for registering a deposit or a withdraw into the transfer proof
Encryption is done using symmetric encryption (using Poseidon2 in duplex mode for ZK friendliness) where the encryption key is derived from the receiving MPC nodes public key.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Compliance is enforced at the protocol level, on-chain. Wallets must register in the Merces contract before transacting, the administrator can blocklist registered wallets to block their transfers, and each transaction passes a Predicate compliance check. Disclosure is likewise protocol-level: a regulator posts an on-chain decryption request from a contract-whitelisted wallet and the MPC network opens the matching shares. The compliance gate is the Merces contract plus TACEO's MPC network.

citations.cited_text: Wallets must be registered in the Merces contract before transacting. Optional KYC or policy checks can be enforced at registration.
Registered wallets can later be restricted or added to a blocklist by the administrator, blocking transfers to or from those accounts.
Transactions undergo a compliance check with our partner Predicate.
we give “regulators” the possibility to request revealing transactions which belong to specific user addresses.
Whenever a match is found, the corresponding shares of the transaction details are opened (i.e., revealed) to the regulator.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: The protocol operator controls enforcement. TACEO operates the MPC network that performs disclosure, and the administrator maintains the wallet registry and the blocklist that gate transfers. Per-transaction screening is delegated to Predicate, but the administrator sets policy and the Merces contract enforces it, with a signed warrant required for disclosure.

citations.cited_text: Registered wallets can later be restricted or added to a blocklist by the administrator, blocking transfers to or from those accounts.
Transactions undergo a compliance check with our partner Predicate.
in a real setup, some form of authentication (e.g., a signed warrant from the regulator) must be provided to the MPC network, such that no-one can misuse this compliance service.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: There are three live mechanisms. Selective disclosure: regulators post an on-chain decryption request and the MPC network opens the matching secret-shared history under a signed warrant. KYC/KYB: wallets must register in the Merces contract before transacting, with optional KYC at registration. Programmatic policies: each transaction undergoes a compliance check via Predicate, and the administrator can blocklist registered wallets. Proof-of-compliance bundles remain in design.

citations.cited_text: Transactions undergo a compliance check with our partner Predicate.
Wallets must be registered in the Merces contract before transacting. Optional KYC or policy checks can be enforced at registration.
Registered wallets can later be restricted or added to a blocklist by the administrator, blocking transfers to or from those accounts.
in a real setup, some form of authentication (e.g., a signed warrant from the regulator) must be provided to the MPC network, such that no-one can misuse this compliance service.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: Compliance gates run at transaction time, not only after the fact. Wallets must register in the Merces contract before transacting, so onboarding with optional KYC gates entry. Each transaction then undergoes a Predicate compliance check, and the administrator can blocklist registered wallets to block transfers to or from them. Selective disclosure remains reactive on top of this: the MPC network reveals a flagged address's transfer details to a regulator after the fact.

citations.cited_text: Transactions undergo a compliance check with our partner Predicate.
Wallets must be registered in the Merces contract before transacting. Optional KYC or policy checks can be enforced at registration.
Registered wallets can later be restricted or added to a blocklist by the administrator, blocking transfers to or from those accounts.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Users retrieve their own balance and history by querying the MPC network directly, which returns the encrypted shares the user combines. For involuntary access, regulators request revelation of transactions for specific addresses, served by the network's secret-shared history searched on demand, with matching shares opened to the regulator and a real setup requiring a signed warrant. No viewing-key delegation to a voluntary third party is part of the construction.

citations.cited_text:
Reading balance 

To read the current balance, a user directly queries the MPC network which responds with the (encrypted) secret-shares of the balance, which can be combined by the users.

Transaction History 

Similar to the compliance service, users can request their transaction history from the MPC network.
we give “regulators” the possibility to request revealing transactions which belong to specific user addresses.
in a real setup, some form of authentication (e.g., a signed warrant from the regulator) must be provided to the MPC network

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: Viewing access is granted retroactively per request rather than fixed at transfer time. Regulators request revelation of transactions for specific addresses, served by the MPC network's secret-shared history searched on request, and whenever a match is found the matching shares are opened to the regulator. Users likewise pull their own history on demand via authenticated requests to the MPC nodes, so permissions are evaluated at query time rather than baked into proofs at transfer time.

citations.cited_text: we give “regulators” the possibility to request revealing transactions which belong to specific user addresses.
Whenever a match is found, the corresponding shares of the transaction details are opened (i.e., revealed) to the regulator.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Each state transition is verified on-chain by a CoSNARK against the prior and new Merkle roots, so a corrupt MPC network cannot forge balances or steal funds. But the protocol rests on the MPC network under an honest-threshold assumption: the current deployment needs a majority of its operators to be honest.

citations.cited_text: every state transition is verified by a zero-knowledge proof onchain. The system runs as a standard Solidity contract, no protocol-level modifications, no custom chain
The MPC network produces a ZK proof attesting for correct evaluation of the next item in the action queue. Once done, it calls processMPC with the new commitment to the Merkle-tree
The current deployment uses REP3 secret sharing: where a majority of operators must cooperate to reconstruct any amount or balance.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The components that implement Merces are open source. The Merces application code — the Solidity smart contracts and the MPC node software — is published in the merces1-paper repository under the MIT licence. The underlying CoSNARK library (co-snarks) is dual-licensed MIT or Apache-2.0, with its co-circom and circom-mpc-compiler sub-libraries under GPL-3.0.

citations.cited_text: Contains the source code of the smart contracts written in solidity.

This project is licensed under either the [MIT License](LICENSE-MIT) or the
[Apache](LICENSE-APACHE), at your choice.

properties.name: Private State Scalability
properties.notes: On-chain state grows with the number of accounts, not transactions. The contract keeps a hashmap from user addresses to balance commitments — one commitment per account, overwritten in place on each update — plus a transient action queue whose entries are deleted after the MPC network processes them. There is no growing nullifier or note set.

citations.cited_text: from user addresses to balance commitments, which serves as the ground truth of user balances.
the UTXO model leads to large growth in blockchain storage and issues with note-discovery.
stores the current commitment (Merkle root) to the encrypted balance tree, and maintains an action queue where users register intents: deposits, transfers, withdrawals.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: The device does not scan the chain. A user discovers their balance by querying the MPC network directly, which returns the encrypted shares the user combines, and transaction history is fetched on request via a linear scan the network performs. Accounts are keyed by an incrementing ID-registry index tied to the user's public key, so lookup is account-keyed. This delegation means the MPC network learns which entity is querying.

citations.cited_text:
Reading balance 

To read the current balance, a user directly queries the MPC network which responds with the (encrypted) secret-shares of the balance, which can be combined by the users.
The MPC network, on request, will thus perform a linear scan of the transactions to be able to reveal the right transactions to the user.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Balances are held as secret shares of a Merkle tree jointly maintained by the TACEO MPC committee, with the contract storing only the Merkle root as the on-chain commitment. The network is built on TACEO's Oblivious Map. Updates follow a per-account model — a transfer deducts the sender's balance and adds it to the receiver — not a note-commitment spend graph, and the committee's shared secret state distinguishes it from a plain encrypted-balance design.

citations.cited_text: three computing nodes that jointly hold secret shares of every user’s balance in a Merkle tree, built on TACEO’s Oblivious Map (OMap)
stores the current commitment (Merkle root) to the encrypted balance tree, and maintains an action queue where users register intents: deposits, transfers, withdrawals.
We deduct the balance of the sender and add them to the receiver

properties.name: Private Data Storage
properties.notes: The contract stores only a commitment to the encrypted balance tree, keeping the current Merkle root and running an action queue for user intents. The authoritative plaintext lives off-chain with the MPC nodes: each party holds secret shares of the full balance Merkle tree, and transaction history is likewise retained off-chain in secret-shared form. On-chain artefacts are limited to commitments, nullifiers, ciphertexts for the nodes, Merkle roots, and proofs registered through the queue.

citations.cited_text: stores the current commitment (Merkle root) to the encrypted balance tree, and maintains an action queue where users register intents: deposits, transfers, withdrawals.
Each party of the MPC network holds secret shares of the full Merkle tree which hold the balances of all users.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.value: Composable interface, but requires DeFi protocol changes
properties.notes: Merces is not plug-and-play with external DeFi, but its design composes by extension. The paper generalises the construction so the MPC network can compute arbitrary functions on private balances — private swaps, lending, yield — each needing a new on-chain verifier and MPC function added to the protocol. Composability therefore exists but requires protocol-level changes per integration and is future work, not live. Touching external DeFi today still means withdrawing back to the public ERC-20.

citations.cited_text: For example, private swaps: the contract holds, per user, multiple balance commitments for different denominations
Extending this to the full range of onchain finance operations (swaps, lending, yield) means solving a set of concrete technical problems
enabling private interactions with external DeFi contracts while preserving the proof model

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Merces is a confidential token-transfer protocol with a fixed operation set — deposits, transfers, and withdrawals of wrapped ERC-20 balances — rather than a general smart-contract platform. The contract keeps the Merkle root and runs an action queue for those intents. Extending to swaps, lending, and yield is future work (see Access to DeFi). The underlying TACEO netork itself supports expressive programmability for private shared state.

citations.cited_text: stores the current commitment (Merkle root) to the encrypted balance tree, and maintains an action queue where users register intents: deposits, transfers, withdrawals.
Extending this to the full range of onchain finance operations (swaps, lending, yield) means solving a set of concrete technical problems
Brings the private-balance model to swap routers, intent flows, and DeFi composability
